symbols: books and writing desk — the life of theological scholarship and the written transmission of faith
study or cell — the contemplative life of the Church Father
Augustine seated in concentration — the labor of understanding and articulating divine truth
bookshelf behind — the accumulated wisdom of the Christian intellectual tradition
description: A small predella panel showing Saint Augustine seated at a writing desk in a study. He wears a dark green robe and bishop's mitre, bent over the desk in intense concentration, writing or reading. Behind him is a bookshelf with several volumes. The study is framed by plain architectural columns and walls in a simple, austere interior. The composition is horizontal and intimate, focusing entirely on the solitary scholar at work.
theological_description: Botticelli's predella panel presents Saint Augustine of Hippo (354-430), the most influential of the Western Church Fathers, in his characteristic role as writer and thinker. Augustine's works — particularly the Confessions and The City of God — shaped Christian theology for centuries. While not a biblical figure, his intellectual labor directly served the mandate Paul gave to Timothy: 'Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth' (2 Timothy 2:15). Augustine's famous conversion came through hearing a child's voice say 'Take up and read' (Tolle Lege), leading him to Romans 13:13-14: 'Put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh.' His theological method exemplified the scriptural principle: 'Come now, let us reason together, says the Lord' (Isaiah 1:18). The study setting embodies the Psalmist's aspiration: 'Oh how I love your law! It is my meditation all the day' (Psalm 119:97). Augustine's intellectual devotion demonstrates that loving God with all one's mind (Matthew 22:37) is itself a form of worship.

scripture_refs.reason: Rightly handling the word of truth — Augustine's lifework of theological scholarship
The text that converted Augustine — putting on the Lord Jesus Christ
Meditating on God's law all day — Augustine's scholarly devotion to Scripture
Loving God with all the mind — intellectual pursuit as a form of devotion
